event: 46 years old
male
Asian-Indian
diagnosed to have tropical chronic pancreatitis
maintained on oral pancreatic enzyme therapy
developed non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus
prescribed glucotrol (glipizide) 5 mg daily
presented to Saint Peter's University Hospital, New Brunswick, NJ
complaining of a steady midepigastric pain of 6 hours duration
dull in nature
radiating through his back
exacerbated by food intake
hospitalized for pain control with narcotics
returned complaining of continuing epigastric pain
associated with nausea
an episode of vomiting
no documented fever
CT scan revealed fatty atrophy of both the pancreatic head and uncinate process
pancreatic duct filled with large calcifications
possible obstruction at the level of the neck
marked upstream ductal dilatation of 1.5 cm
admitted for management of pain secondary to chronic pancreatitis
presented to a medical center in India with similar pain and fever
underwent a CT scan of the abdomen
chronic atrophic calcific pancreatitis
dilated pancreatic duct (7 mm)
intraductal calculi
endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) was performed
medically managed
discharged to return for removal of ductal stones
developed fever (102°F)
chills and rigors
rapidly progressed to severe sepsis and septic shock
transferred to the medical intensive care unit
intubated for hypoxemic respiratory failure
clinical parameters were consistent with acute respiratory distress syndrome
multiorgan failure
administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics
vasopressor support
activated recombinant human protein C
ultrasound of the abdomen was done
markedly dilated pancreatic duct (1.9 cm)
8.7 × 7.6 mm calculus within the duct
pancreas was diffusely echogenic
common bile duct was prominent with no obvious obstructing calculi
bedside emergency ERCP was performed
major papilla of Vater was visualized spontaneously expelling frank pus
no evidence of papillitis
tumor or previous sphincterotomy
evacuation of more than 5 ml of yellow pus
cholangiogram was obtained revealing a normal biliary tree without stones
pancreatogram showed marked dilatation of the main pancreatic duct
single distal calculus
total of approximately 20 ml of pus was then evacuated during duct manipulation
achieving selective cannulation
5-cm-long 5 F stent was placed into the pancreatic duct
failed to show any more pus draining
decision was taken to pull it out
assumed that the patient had an ASPD
reevaluation of the pancreas with a contrast enhanced CT scan of the abdomen
inflammatory changes within the fat surrounding the body and tail of the pancreas
edema
dilatation of the pancreatic duct had diminished
calculus showing distal migration towards the sphincter of Oddi
no evidence of pancreatic necrosis or fluid collection
bilateral moderate pleural effusions were present
showed dramatic signs of clinical improvement
stabilization of his hemodynamic parameters
extubated
transferred from the intensive care unit
completed his antibiotic course
discharged home 11 days after admission
follow-up examinations at 1 month and 3 months after discharge revealed no further complications
